perturbation.original_text: Distributor will provide IMNTV with a quarterly report or monthly report if available, summarizing the data relevant to the payment obligations herein within five (5) business days from the end of the calendar quarter.
perturbation.changed_text: Distributor will provide IMNTV with a quarterly report, summarizing the data relevant to the payment obligations herein within thirty (60) business days from the end of the calendar quarter.
perturbation.explanation: The original text requires the distributor to provide IMNTV with a quarterly report within five business days from the end of the calendar quarter. Changing the deadline to sixty business days contradicts standard accounting practices and could violate regulations requiring timely and accurate financial reporting.
perturbation.law_explanation: This law emphasizes the importance of internal controls and accurate financial reporting. A 60-day reporting window after each quarter-end may render financial data untimely and unreliable.

perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the reporting deadline from five business days to sixty business days. The Law Explanation states this "may render financial data untimely and unreliable." Scraped Law Snippet 1, 15 USC 7241, discusses "Corporate responsibility for financial reports" and requires companies filing periodic reports to have principal officers certify these reports. A 60-day delay undermines the timeliness expected for accurate financial reporting, potentially conflicting with the requirement for responsible financial reporting and internal controls implied by 15 USC 7241.

perturbation.original_text: Either party may terminate the Agreement at any time in the event of a material breach by the other party that remains uncured after thirty (30) days written notice of the breach.
perturbation.changed_text: Either party may terminate the Agreement at any time in the event of a material breach by the other party that remains uncured after ten (10) days written notice of the breach.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for a material breach from thirty (30) days to ten (10) days may be considered unreasonable and conflict with legal principles requiring fair opportunity to cure contractual defaults. This is especially relevant if the agreement is silent on the definition of a 'material breach'.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Contract Law - Reasonableness of Notice Periods
perturbation.law_explanation: UCC 2-309 requires that termination clauses provide reasonable notification. Ten days may be viewed as insufficient time for a party to rectify a material breach, especially for complex issues. What counts as 'reasonable' would depend on the circumstances, including industry practice and the nature of the breach.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. | Uniform Commercial Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you § 2-309. Absence of Specific Time Provisions; Notice of Termination. (1) The time for shipment or delivery or any other action under a contract if not provided in this Article or agreed upon shall be a reasonable time. (2) Where the contract provides for successive performances but is indefinite in duration it is valid for a reasonable time but unless otherwise agreed may be terminated at any time by either party. (3) Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party and an agreement dispensing with notification is invalid if its operation would be unconscionable. Uniform Commercial Code Toolbox About Uniform Laws State Uniform Commercial Codes
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the cure period for a material breach from 30 days to 10 days, which creates a contradiction with UCC 2-309. The Law Explanation states that UCC 2-309 requires reasonable notification for termination clauses. Scraped Law Snippet 1 supports this, stating, 'Termination of a contract by one party except on the happening of an agreed event requires that reasonable notification be received by the other party.' The changed text reduces the cure period and thus may not be considered 'reasonable notification,' especially for complex issues, as highlighted in the Law Explanation. This contradiction is direct and impactful, potentially nullifying protections intended by the law.

perturbation.original_text: From the Effective Date and for a period of two (2) years from the date of termination or expiration of this Agreement, neither party will use, disclose, or permit any person to obtain any Confidential Information of the other party.
perturbation.changed_text: From the Effective Date and until termination or expiration of this Agreement, neither party will use, disclose, or permit any person to obtain any Confidential Information of the other party.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the two-year confidentiality period after termination contradicts established intellectual property and trade secret law which generally allows for protection of confidential information for a reasonable period even after a business relationship ends. This removal effectively allows the receiving party to immediately use or disclose confidential information upon termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA protects trade secrets, and while the definition of a trade secret is broad, confidential business information covered by the agreement could qualify. Removing the post-termination confidentiality period weakens the protections afforded to such information, making it easier for a party to misappropriate and use it.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The original text ensures confidentiality for two years post-termination, while the changed text eliminates this post-termination protection. This weakens the protection of trade secrets. According to the Law Explanation, the DTSA protects trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 18 USC 1836, allows for civil actions by the owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated. Removing the post-termination confidentiality period in the changed text makes it easier for a party to misappropriate confidential information, undermining the protection offered by the DTSA and thus creating a contradiction.
